subject: Ecology
title: Wetland Soils
description: A tiny lesson with a big payoff: Wetland Soils.

hook.text: Wetland soils are dark, gooey, and smell like rotten eggs—and that's a sign of health.

buildup.text: Waterlogged soil has no oxygen. Anaerobic bacteria produce hydrogen sulfide—the rotten-egg gas.

discovery.text: These oxygen-free conditions slow decomposition, allowing organic matter to accumulate as peat.

twist.text: Wetland soils are called hydric soils. Their distinct color—gray, blue, or black—reveals the chemistry.

climax.text: Hydric soils can store enormous amounts of carbon—wetlands are Earth's most efficient carbon sinks.

punchline.text: Smelly soil stores more carbon than clean soil.

quiz.question: Why do wetland soils accumulate organic matter?
options: Sand prevents matter from washing away
